label: vLLM on a GPU
subject: Why vLLM batches (GPU inference throughput)
bossHook: Six requests, one GPU, limited VRAM. Serve them in as few batches as possible without an out-of-memory — that packing is why vLLM is fast.

vocab.hint: Tap a request to move it between batches. Pack each batch as full as VRAM allows — fewer batches = higher throughput.

warmup.title: One GPU, Many Requests
warmup.hook: A naive server runs ONE request at a time. Your GPU can hold several at once — drop them into a single batch and it serves them together in one pass.
warmup.winText: Batched together, the GPU did in one pass what a naive server needs three for. That single idea is most of vLLM's speed.

learn.frame: The GPU can hold several requests at once — so why would a server ever run them one at a time?

reveal.body: Every forward pass pays a big fixed cost — launched once whether it carries one request or many. Group them into a single batch and that cost is paid ONCE for all of them. That's BATCHING.
reveal.inTheWild: vLLM pushes many prompts through one forward pass — the fixed GPU cost amortized across the whole batch.

pack-tight.title: Keep the GPU Full
pack-tight.hook: Five requests, VRAM for 8 blocks a batch. Run them one-per-batch and the GPU idles between each. Pack each batch to the brim instead.
pack-tight.winText: Two full batches instead of five thin ones — the GPU stayed busy the whole time. That's continuous batching.

failText.underutilize: You spread the requests too thin — the GPU sat idle between tiny batches. Pack each batch fuller and the batch count drops.
failText.overcommit: That batch asked for more VRAM than the GPU has — an out-of-memory. Move a request to another batch.

reveal.body: The GPU idled between thin batches because each pass costs the same no matter how full it is. Packing to the brim spreads that fixed cost over more requests — fewer, fuller passes. That's higher THROUGHPUT.
reveal.inTheWild: Continuous batching keeps the GPU saturated — new requests slot into a running batch instead of waiting their turn.

insights.underutilize: The pass pays its fixed launch cost whether it carries one request or eight. A thin batch spreads that over too few, so cost-per-request stays high — full batches are what amortize it.
insights.overcommit: VRAM is a hard wall, not a target. One block over and the batch won't launch at all, so overpacking buys nothing — pack up to the line, then split into another batch.

respect-memory.title: VRAM Is the Ceiling
respect-memory.hook: One big request needs 5 of 6 blocks. Batch greedily and you'll overflow VRAM. Pack tight — but never past the ceiling.
respect-memory.winText: Every batch full yet under the VRAM line. Throughput AND no OOM — keeping memory packed but safe is exactly PagedAttention's job.

failText.underutilize: Fewer, fuller batches would serve these faster — the GPU idled. But mind the ceiling as you pack.
failText.overcommit: A batch blew past VRAM. When a request is large, give it room — split the batch so nothing overflows.

reveal.body: Pack tight, but VRAM is a hard ceiling — a batch can't hold more blocks than the GPU has. The move is to fill each batch right up to the limit and no further.
reveal.inTheWild: PagedAttention pages the KV-cache into fixed blocks, so vLLM can pack VRAM to the edge without overflowing.

insights.underutilize: Unused VRAM is throughput left on the table — the pass runs either way, so blocks you didn't fill are requests you didn't serve this round.
insights.overcommit: Exceeding VRAM doesn't slow the batch, it kills it — an OOM drops the whole thing. The ceiling is the real constraint: pack up to it, never through it.

boss-serve.title: BOSS · Serve the Queue
boss-serve.hook: Six requests, VRAM for 8, and three batches to stay ahead of the queue. Pack them tight and keep every batch under the ceiling.
boss-serve.winText: Six requests served in three packed batches, no OOM. That's a vLLM scheduler doing its job — high throughput, memory-safe.

failText.underutilize: Too many batches — the GPU idled and the queue backed up. Pack the batches fuller.
failText.overcommit: A batch overflowed VRAM. Rebalance the requests so each batch fits under the ceiling.

learn.frame: Fewer batches means more throughput, but too big a batch OOMs — so where exactly does the best batch sit?

reveal.body: Maximum THROUGHPUT lives at the tightest packing the VRAM ceiling allows — one block under the limit, never over. Too loose wastes the GPU; too tight overflows it. The win is right at the edge.
reveal.inTheWild: A vLLM scheduler does exactly this every step — pack VRAM to the max, stay memory-safe, keep the queue moving.

insights.underutilize: Fixed launch cost per pass means fewer, fuller batches always win — an idle GPU is throughput you can't get back.
insights.overcommit: The memory ceiling is absolute: a single batch over the line OOMs and serves nothing. Peak throughput sits right below the limit, never above it.

batch-drill.title: SIDE-QUEST · Fill One Batch
batch-drill.hook: Drill the core habit: three small requests, one batch, VRAM enough for all of them. Put them ALL in batch 1.
batch-drill.winText: All three in a single batch — the maximum-throughput move. Batch first, always.

reveal.body: When everything fits, one batch is always the move — one pass, fixed cost paid once, every request served together. Batch first.
reveal.inTheWild: The default instinct in any throughput system: group the work before you run it.
